REAL PROPERTY/HOUSING MOTION 501, DEFENDANTS EMILIE CALHOUN AND FIDUCIARY LAW ATTORNEYS' MOTION FOR RELIEF BECAUSE OF MISTAKE, SURPRISE, OR EXCUSABLE NEGLECT PURSUANT TO CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE SECTION 473(B) IS DENIED. THE NOTICE OF MOTION FILED ON DECEMBER 29, 2023 REQUESTS "THE THAT THE COURT CONSIDER DEFENDANT'S LATE-FILED AND SERVED INDEX AND EXHIBITS IN SUPPORT OF THEIR MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION." THE ARGUMENT SECTION OF THE POINTS AND AUTHORITIES ALSO DISCUSSED "INDEX OF EXHIBITS AND EXHIBITS." THEREFORE, ANY REQUEST OF RELIEF FROM LATE FILING OF THE SEPARATE STATEMENT (AND OTHER DOCUMENTS FILED ON DECEMBER 16, 2023) RAISED FOR THE FIRST TIME IN THE REPLY IS NOT PROPERLY BEFORE THE COURT AND WILL NOT BE CONSIDERED. AS TO THE LATE FILING OF THE INDEX OF EXHIBITS AND EXHIBITS IN SUPPORT OF THE MOVING PAPERS, MOVING PARTY CITES NO AUTHORITY FOR THE PROPOSITION THAT SUCH RELIEF IS AVAILABLE. KAPITANSKI V. VON'S GROCERY CO., INC. (1983) 146 CAL.APP.3D 29 AND WEISS V. CHEVRON, U.S.A., INC. (1988) 204 CAL.APP.3D. 1094 ARE FACTUALLY AND PROCEDURALLY DISTINGUISHABLE. THE HEARING ON THE MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT REMAINS ON CALENDAR. IT IS WITHIN THE COURT'S DISCRETION TO CONSIDER LATE-FILED SUBMISSIONS IN SUPPORT OF TIMELY FILED NOTICE OF MOTION. IT IS ALSO THE PLAINTIFF'S RIGHT TO OBJECT TO ANY FILINGS NOT IN PROCEDURAL COMPLIANCE WITH CCP 437C. ROBINSON V. WOODS (2008) 168 CAL.APP.4TH 1258. THE COURT WILL DETERMINE WHETHER IT WILL CONSIDER THE FILINGS IN CONJUNCTION WITH THE HEARING ON THE MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT HAVING REVIEWED THE OPPOSITION (IF ANY) AND REPLY (IF ANY). THE COURT NOTES THAT THE MOVING PARTY FAILED TO PROVIDE COURTESY COPIES OF ITS MOVING PAPERS TO DEPARTMENT 501 (OPPOSITION AND REPLY RECEIVED). ORDER SIGNED. JUDGE: CHARLES F. HAINES; CLERK: VANESSA WU; REPORTED. (D501)

Motion To Strike And/Or Exclude Defts' Late Disclosed Non-Retained Expert Testimony
Off Calendar Mar-22-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFFS PATRICIA DARK, MARY CONNOLLY'S Motion To Strike And/Or Exclude Defts' Late Disclosed Non-Retained Expert Testimony

In this legal malpractice action, on March 4, 2024 defendants served a supplemental designation of expert witnesses pursuant to CCP section 2034.280 listing two retained experts and one non-retained expert. On March 8, 2024, plaintiffs filed a motion to strike the non-retained expert witness on the ground that the code does not allow supplementing an expert witness list with a non-retained expert. Plaintiffs also contended that the supplemental disclosure would cause prejudice because they would be unable to depose the non-retained expert before trial. The motion was noticed for hearing on March 22, 2024.

In response, defendants argued that plaintiffs' motion was improperly noticed, providing only ten court days' notice instead of the sixteen days required under CCP section 1005(b). Evidently recognizing the legitimacy of this objection, on March 13, plaintiffs filed a request for an order shortening time so that their motion to strike could be heard on March 22, 2024. That application was denied by the Court on March 14, 2024.

As to the merits of the motion, none of the authority cited by plaintiffs in their motion supports their contention Section 2034.280 only allows supplementing an expert disclosure with retained experts. There is also the question whether a motion to strike is the proper
means of raising an objection of this kind. Moreover, Section 2034.280(c) provides that the supplementing party must make its "new" experts available for deposition without regard to the closing of discovery prior to trial. That said, in their supplemental disclosure, defendants provided the information called for by Section 2034.280 for the retained experts but nothing for the non-retained expert. Presumably defendants had communicated with the non-retained expert in order to determine whether his testimony would assist their defense of the case. But they provided no outline of that expected testimony in their disclosure, making it impossible to determine whether his testimony would indeed be supplementary to defendants' initial expert disclosure under Section 2034.260(b).

In any event, the motion to strike the non-retained expert must be DENIED for failure to provide the proper notice under Section 1005(b). As the Court recited on March 14, 2024, this matter may be raised again with the assigned trial judge. That may also provide the opportunity to offer a motion in limine (as was done in Du-All Safety, LLC v. Superior Court (Krein), 34 Cal.App.5th 485 (2019)) rather than a motion to strike.

The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312 and e-mail the proposed order to the Judge Pro Tem Philip Ward at psw@hassard.com.

Judge Pro Tem: Philip Ward; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/JPT)

DEFENDANTS EMILIE CALHOUN AND FIDUCIARY LAW ATTORNEYS' MOTION FOR RELIEF BECAUSE OF MISTAKE, SURPRISE, OR EXCUSABLE NEGLECT PURSUANT TO CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE SECTION 473(B)
Off Calendar Jan-23-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: In-Person Appearances:

Laura Grealish Esq., Murphy, Pearson, Bradley & Feeney; 415-788-1900; appearance for defendants
David Helbraun Esq., Helbraun Law Firm; 415-982-4000; appearance for plaintiffs
Peggy Tsujimoto, CSR 5229, 415-828-1919, peggytsujimoto@gmail.com

Mr. David Helbraun contests the tentative. After reviewing the pleadings and listening to oral arguments the court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS EMILIE CALHOUN AND FIDUCIARY LAW ATTORNEYS' MOTION FOR RELIEF BECAUSE OF MISTAKE, SURPRISE, OR EXCUSABLE NEGLECT PURSUANT TO CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE SECTION 473(B) is DENIED. The Notice of Motion filed on December 29, 2023 requests "the that the Court consider Defendant's late-filed and served index and exhibits in support of their Motion for Summary Judgment, or in the alternative, Summary Adjudication." The argument section of the Points and Authorities also discussed "index of exhibits and exhibits." Therefore, any request of relief from late filing of the separate statement (and other documents filed on December 16, 2023) raised for the first time in the reply is not properly before the Court and will not be considered.
As to the late filing of the Index of Exhibits and Exhibits in support of the moving papers, moving party cites no authority for the proposition that such relief is available. Kapitanski v. Von's Grocery Co., Inc. (1983) 146 Cal.App.3d 29 and Weiss v. Chevron, U.S.A., Inc. (1988) 204 Cal.App.3d. 1094 are factually and procedurally distinguishable.
The hearing on the motion for summary judgment remains on calendar. It is within the Court's discretion to consider late-filed submissions in support of timely filed Notice of Motion. It is also the Plaintiff's right to object to any filings not in procedural compliance with CCP 437c. Robinson v. Woods (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 1258. The Court will determine whether it will consider the filings in conjunction with the hearing on the Motion for Summary Judgment having reviewed the opposition (if any) and reply (if any).
The Court notes that the moving party failed to provide courtesy copies of its moving papers to department 501 (opposition and reply received).

Order signed. Judge: Charles F. Haines; Clerk: Vanessa Wu; Reported. (D501)
